planting.note: Vigorous smooth-leaf hybrid that produces heavy yields of dark green leaves.

directions: Plant seed a half inch deep in fertile ground and keep evenly watered for fast, uniform emergence. The thick, deep-green leaves stand upright, staying clean and easy to cut. Harvest baby leaves early or full leaves later, working through the crop before warm weather.

planting.note: A large-leaved French heirloom prized for productivity in cool weather.

directions: Sow directly in cool spring or autumn soil, spacing generously to accommodate the big leaves. This old variety produces very large, tender smooth leaves that grow quickly with steady moisture and rich ground. Pick outer leaves continuously or harvest whole plants before they bolt in warming weather.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant savoy strain selected for overwintering and early spring cuts.

directions: Sow in late summer so plants root well before cold arrives, or in earliest spring for a quick crop. The dark, blistered leaves shrug off frost and resume growth when temperatures ease. Take outer leaves through the cool months and clear the bed before flower stalks form.

planting.note: A red-veined hybrid grown mainly as a colorful baby-leaf salad spinach.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed thickly into cool, moist soil for a dense baby-leaf stand. The smooth green leaves carry striking red veins that brighten salad mixes. Cut leaves young with scissors while the color is vivid, and resow every couple of weeks for a steady supply.

planting.note: A smooth-leaf hybrid valued for vigor and adaptability in spring or fall.

directions: Sow a half inch deep in fertile, cool soil and keep it damp until seedlings appear. This adaptable variety produces round, dark leaves quickly and resists bolting reasonably well. Harvest by cutting outer leaves or lifting whole rosettes before hot days arrive.

planting.note: An upright semi-savoy hybrid with good mildew resistance for multiple seasons.

directions: Drill seed into cool, moist soil and thin as plants develop. The erect habit and lightly savoyed leaves keep foliage clean and simplify cutting for baby leaf or bunching. Harvest young leaves for salads or grow on for larger cooking greens, finishing before warm weather.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant hybrid useful for pushing spinach later into warm weather.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool to mild soil and keep moisture steady to slow bolting. This semi-savoy hybrid tolerates warmth better than most, extending the harvest into early summer. Cut leaves regularly while young and tender, and provide light afternoon shade in the warmest plantings.

planting.note: A baby-leaf hybrid with rounded, upright leaves that stay clean in dense sowings.

directions: Sow seed thickly and shallowly for a full baby-leaf carpet in cool weather. The compact, upright leaves resist splashing soil and cut cleanly with scissors. Harvest at the small-leaf stage for salad blends and make repeat sowings to keep the supply coming.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost for a crop that produces all season into fall.

directions: Soak the crinkly seed clusters overnight, then sow about three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the multiple seedlings that emerge. This classic white-stemmed chard grows large, glossy, heavily savoyed leaves and tolerates both heat and light frost. Harvest outer stalks by twisting them off at the base, leaving the center to keep producing.

planting.note: A rainbow-stemmed mix grown as much for ornament as for eating.

directions: Sow the seed clusters about half an inch deep and thin seedlings to give each plant room. Stems emerge in shades of gold, pink, orange, and crimson, making it a fine choice for edible borders. Pick baby leaves early or harvest outer stalks continuously once plants are established.

planting.note: Deep crimson stems and veins make this heirloom both edible and decorative.

directions: Sow clusters three-quarters of an inch deep in fertile soil and thin the crowded seedlings. The brilliant red stalks and bronze-green leaves hold their color best in cool weather and full sun. Harvest outer stems as they reach usable size and keep plants watered to avoid stringy growth.

scientific_name: Beta vulgaris subsp. vulgaris 'Five Color Silverbeet'

planting.note: An Australian heirloom mix with stems in five distinct colors.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin to a single strong plant per spot. Each plant may show red, pink, yellow, orange, or white stems, so a row makes a colorful display. Cut outer leaves and stalks regularly, and the plant will produce from spring through hard frost.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant white-stemmed heirloom favored in warm summer regions.

directions: Sow clusters three-quarters of an inch deep and thin the seedlings once they stand a few inches tall. This vigorous variety keeps producing pale green, crumpled leaves even through summer heat. Harvest outer stalks often to encourage a steady flush of tender new leaves.

planting.note: A thin-stemmed chard that mimics spinach and rarely bolts in summer.

directions: Sow the seed clusters half an inch deep and thin the seedlings to give room. Unlike true spinach, this leaf beet keeps making mild, tender leaves through heat without running to seed. Pick outer leaves as a spinach substitute and the plant will produce for many months.

planting.note: A vivid pink-to-magenta stemmed selection good for baby leaf or full stalks.

directions: Sow half an inch deep and thin as seedlings crowd, or leave dense for baby leaf. The bright magenta stems and green leaves brighten both the garden and the plate. Harvest young leaves early or wait for full-sized stalks, cutting the outer ones first.

planting.note: A candy-striped selection with pink-and-white banded stems.

directions: Sow seed clusters half an inch deep and thin to give each plant space. The stems show a striking pink-and-white candy stripe that stands out in ornamental beds. Harvest outer stalks as needed, and pick some young for the brightest striping.

planting.note: A golden-stemmed selection that holds its color better than mixed strains.

directions: Sow the seed three-quarters of an inch deep and thin seedlings once established. Its glowing yellow stems contrast with dark green leaves and keep their color in cooking better than most. Harvest outer stalks regularly to keep the plant producing tender new growth.

planting.note: The classic green mountain spinach, more heat-tolerant than true spinach.

directions: Direct sow half an inch deep once the soil warms slightly and keep it moist for even germination. This vigorous annual makes a tall column of soft, mild leaves that stay tender longer than spinach in heat. Harvest young leaves regularly and cut the tips to encourage bushy regrowth and delay flowering.

planting.note: A yellow-green leaved form grown for both salads and ornamental color.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in cool soil and thin the seedlings to give room to grow tall. The chartreuse to golden foliage brightens the vegetable patch and works well in edible arrangements. Harvest tender young leaves and remove seed heads to keep the plant leafy through the season.

planting.note: A richly colored purple-red orach selection for salads and cut foliage.

directions: Direct sow half an inch deep in early spring and thin plants to stand a foot or more apart. The deep magenta leaves add bold color to salad bowls and hold well when cut for arrangements. Pick leaves young and pinch back flowering tips to keep fresh growth coming.

subcategory: leafy vining perennial grown as annual

planting.note: A tropical vine that thrives in summer heat once all frost is past.

directions: Nick or soak the hard seed and start indoors, then transplant to a trellis after the weather turns reliably warm. This vigorous vine climbs quickly and produces thick, glossy leaves that stay mild through summer heat. Pinch growing tips to encourage branching and harvest young leaves and shoots continuously until frost.

subcategory: leafy vining perennial grown as annual

planting.note: A red-stemmed tropical vine grown for both eating and ornamental value.

directions: Soak seed overnight and start indoors in warm conditions, transplanting to a sturdy trellis after the soil warms. The striking red stems and dark green leaves make an attractive edible screen in summer. Keep the roots moist, pinch tips for bushiness, and harvest tender shoots and leaves right up to frost.

planting.note: An old-fashioned green grown for both spinach-like leaves and red berry clusters.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool spring soil and thin the seedlings once they establish. The triangular leaves eat much like spinach when young, and later the plant sets edible red mulberry-like clusters. Harvest leaves early and pick the mild berries as they redden along the stems.

planting.note: Sow in spring for greens or in midsummer for a fall crop of tender leaves.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in loosened soil and thin seedlings once they crowd. Keep the bed evenly moist to keep the leaves from turning too bitter in heat. Cut young rosette leaves for salad or let the plant grow on and blanch the heart before harvest.

planting.note: Sow in mid to late summer so heads firm up during cooling autumn weather.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin plants to give each room to form a round head. Cool nights turn the leaves deep red and tighten the ball, so time the crop for fall. Harvest when the head feels firm and dense, cutting just above the base to allow a second flush of leaves.

planting.note: Start in summer for a dependable fall harvest of ball-shaped heads.

directions: Sow directly and thin to a hand's width apart for well-filled heads. This reliable heading type reddens as temperatures drop, so keep it well watered through late summer. Harvest firm globes before hard freezes, and strip away any loose outer green leaves.

planting.note: Sow in summer; this upright type sweetens after light frosts.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to leave room for the loose, upright head to develop. The elongated wine-red leaves with white ribs color best once nights turn cold. Cut whole plants after a frost or two, when the flavor has mellowed from sharp to pleasantly bitter.

planting.note: Sow in summer; this late strain is traditionally lifted, blanched, and forced after frost.

directions: Sow directly and grow the plants on into cold weather. After several frosts, lift the roots and hold them in the dark so the inner leaves regrow blanched, curled, and tender. Trim the forced shoots when the crisp red and white leaves have curled back on themselves.

planting.note: Sow in late summer for small, tight fall heads.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin plants so each can form a compact rosette. Cold weather deepens the red color and firms the small oval heads. Harvest after frost when the hearts feel solid, peeling off any tattered outer leaves.

planting.note: Sow in summer; cold weather brings out the speckled coloring.

directions: Sow directly and thin plants to allow the loose rose-shaped head to open. The pale green-yellow leaves develop their signature red speckles only after cold sets in. Harvest the open, flower-like heads once they have colored up following frost.

planting.note: Sow in late spring to grow storage roots, then force them in darkness for winter chicons.

directions: Sow directly and grow the plants all season to build up thick taproots. In late fall lift the roots, trim the tops, and pack them upright in a dark, cool bin kept damp. The buried crowns push up tight, pale, torpedo-shaped chicons over a few weeks that you cut when several inches tall.

planting.note: Sow in late summer for a fall crop of hollow, asparagus-like shoots.

directions: Sow directly and thin to give the toothed, upright leaves room. Cool weather encourages the plant to send up its edible hollow flower stalks from the center. Cut the tender young shoots and inner leaves before the stems toughen and flower.

planting.note: Sow thickly in spring or fall for quick, mild baby-leaf cutting greens.

directions: Broadcast or sow in close rows for a dense stand of tender green leaves. This sweet cutting type is grown young, so keep it moist and harvest early. Shear the leaves a couple inches above the soil while small, and the bed will regrow for several cuts.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; despite the name it is a chicory, not a true dandelion.

directions: Sow directly and thin to give the deeply notched leaves room to lengthen. The dark green blades ride on bright red midribs that stand upright as the plant matures. Pick outer leaves young for salads or let them size up and cook the older greens.

planting.note: Sow in close rows for repeated cuttings of smooth green leaves.

directions: Sow densely and keep evenly watered for a lush stand of narrow, sword-shaped leaves. This smooth-leaved cutting chicory is harvested young for its mild, slightly bitter flavor. Shear the leaves above the growing point and the plants will flush again for further cuts.

scientific_name: Cichorium endivia var. crispum 'Très Fine Maraîchère'

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; blanch the center to reduce bitterness before harvest.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin plants to form full, finely cut rosettes. About two weeks before harvest, tie the outer leaves up or set a plate over the center to blanch the heart pale and mild. Cut the whole rosette at the base once the middle has whitened and the frilly leaves are crisp.

scientific_name: Cichorium endivia var. crispum 'Green Curled Ruffec'

planting.note: Sow in summer for a hardy, deeply frilled fall crop.

directions: Sow directly and thin plants so the deeply cut, mossy leaves can fill out. This vigorous frisée tolerates cool weather well, making it a good fall grower. Blanch the heart under a cover for a week or two, then cut the rosette when the center is creamy and tender.

planting.note: Sow in spring or midsummer; broad flat leaves take light frost well.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin plants to give the broad, slightly wavy leaves plenty of room. Escarole is less bitter than curly endive and forms a loose heart of pale inner leaves. Blanch the center under a cover before harvest, then cut the head at the base when the middle has lightened.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant; forms large, well-filled self-folding hearts.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out sturdy transplants after hardening off. This broad-leaved escarole naturally folds its outer leaves over a large blanched heart, easing the usual tying. Harvest the full head when the center is dense and creamy pale.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant for uniform, self-blanching frisée heads.

directions: Start seed in cells and transplant well-rooted plugs into fertile soil. This finely cut endive fills in densely and blanches much of its own center as the head closes. Harvest the crisp rosette once the heart has paled, cutting cleanly at the base.

planting.note: Sow in spring; this selection makes a fuller, more upright rosette than the wild type.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin plants to allow the dense heart to develop. This improved French dandelion produces a thicker, more abundant clump of leaves than roadside plants. Blanch the center under a pot for a week or two before cutting for tender, less bitter greens.

planting.note: Start indoors in summer and transplant for a uniform Chioggia-type fall crop.

directions: Start seed in cells and set out transplants for even spacing and reliable heading. This hybrid Chioggia type forms dense round red-and-white heads with good field holding. Harvest firm balls in the cool of fall, trimming away the green wrapper leaves.

planting.note: Start indoors in summer and transplant for firm, early-maturing heads.

directions: Start transplants in midsummer and set them out into fertile, well-drained soil. This vigorous hybrid forms tight, deep-red round heads and resists bolting in warm spells. Harvest when the heads feel solid, cutting at the base and peeling off loose outer leaves.

planting.note: Start indoors in summer; this Treviso-type forms upright torpedo heads.

directions: Start seed in cells and transplant seedlings for an even, upright stand. This Treviso-style hybrid makes elongated red heads with crisp white ribs that color with cold. Cut the whole head after light frosts have sweetened the leaves.

planting.note: Sow once soil is thoroughly warm and all frost danger has passed.

directions: Scatter the tiny seeds barely below the surface and keep them evenly moist until they sprout. Thin seedlings so the strongest stand a few inches apart, or grow densely for cut-and-come-again baby leaves. Begin picking tender young leaves and shoot tips when plants reach several inches tall, harvesting before the summer heat pushes them to flower.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil and make successions every few weeks for a steady supply.

directions: Press seed just under the surface into warm, weed-free soil and water gently. Harvest whole young plants or pinch leafy tips to encourage branching and fresh regrowth. Cook the mild greens like spinach; keep cutting through summer to delay flowering and keep the leaves tender.

planting.note: Grow in spring or fall; this hybrid resists bolting better than most.

directions: Direct sow in cool soil or start transplants, setting plants a hand's width apart. Water steadily and feed well so the white petioles stay thick and crisp. Cut the whole head at the base when the stalks are plump, or take outer stalks as you need them.

scientific_name: Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis 'Mei Qing Choi'

planting.note: A compact green-stemmed baby type; sow in succession for tender heads.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to a few inches for small single-serving heads. Keep the bed moist and harvest young to enjoy the mild flavor and pale green stems. Cut the compact heads whole while still tight, before warm weather triggers flowering.

planting.note: Grows best as a fall crop; spring sowings often bolt in heat.

directions: Sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest, thinning to give heads room to form. Water consistently and feed generously so the tall cylindrical heads fill in densely. Harvest when the heads feel firm; cool weather improves both sweetness and storage life.

planting.note: Fast, cold-tolerant, and slow to bolt; sow spring through fall.

directions: Scatter seed shallowly and thin for large fringed clumps or grow thick for baby salad leaves. Keep soil evenly moist to hold the mild peppery flavor and feathery texture. Cut leaves as a cut-and-come-again crop; plants regrow several times and stand well into frost.

planting.note: A fast flowering-stem green; harvest at the yellow-bud stage.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and thin to a few inches apart for quick, upright plants. Keep well watered so the slender stems and leaves stay succulent. Snap or cut the flowering stalks when the first yellow buds appear, and lower buds will push more shoots.

planting.note: A sprawling heat-tolerant spinach substitute; sow after frost.

directions: Soak the knobby seed clusters overnight and sow about half an inch deep once soil warms. The plant sprawls into a dense mat and shrugs off summer heat that bolts ordinary spinach. Pinch off the tender leafy tips regularly, and new shoots keep coming through the whole warm season.

planting.note: A succulent heat-lover; sow on warm soil and barely cover the seed.

directions: Press the fine seed onto warm soil and keep the surface damp until it sprouts. The low succulent plant thrives in heat and lean ground with little fuss. Snip the tangy, juicy stems and leaves as they grow; cutting above the base keeps fresh shoots coming all summer.

planting.note: A fast legume grown for its aromatic leaves; sow densely and often.

directions: Broadcast the seed thickly and cover lightly, watering to keep it evenly moist. Growth is quick, so sow in succession every couple of weeks for a steady supply. Cut the young clover-like greens whole when a few inches tall, before the plants toughen and set seed pods.

planting.note: Start indoors for an early crop; the ruffled green form is prized in Japanese cooking.

directions: Surface-sow the light-dependent seed indoors and transplant after the weather warms. Pinch the growing tips often to keep the plant bushy and full of fragrant leaves. Harvest individual leaves as needed and remove flower spikes to keep new foliage coming, though it self-seeds freely.

planting.note: Sow indoors roughly ten weeks before setting out, then transplant after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors in flats and barely cover it, keeping the surface moist and warm until germination. Move sturdy seedlings to fertile, well-watered ground and mulch to hold moisture around the roots. This green upright type produces long, crisp, dark stalks; cut the head at soil level once bunches are full and firm.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors about ten weeks ahead and transplant once soil warms.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a fine seed mix and keep it lit and evenly moist to break dormancy. This dwarf yellow-hearted type pales its own inner stalks, though close spacing improves blanching further. Water generously through the season and harvest the compact bunches before autumn frost hardens them.

planting.note: Sow indoors about ten weeks early and set out after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and hold it moist and warm until seedlings appear over two to three weeks. This vigorous, more heat- and drought-forgiving type still rewards steady watering with tender, aromatic stalks. Harvest the whole plant or peel outer stalks as needed once they thicken.

planting.note: Start indoors ten weeks before the last frost and transplant when settled weather arrives.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist mix and keep it evenly damp and lit until it sprouts. This red-stalked selection colors up most deeply in cool weather and rich, well-fed soil. Water consistently to keep the ribs tender and cut the bunch at the base when stalks are full-sized and blushed.

planting.note: Sow indoors early or direct-sow around the last frost; it tolerates cool weather well.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it moist until seedlings emerge, thinning or transplanting to a hand's width apart. Grown for its intensely flavored leaves and thin stems rather than fat stalks, it is far easier than stalk celery and stands light frost. Snip outer stems repeatedly through the season and the plant keeps producing.

planting.note: Sow indoors ten to twelve weeks ahead and set out once frost has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of moist mix and be patient through slow, uneven germination. This old large-rooted variety needs a long, evenly watered season in fertile ground to bulk up its round roots. Remove lower leaves and soil from the crown in late summer, then harvest big smooth roots in autumn.

planting.note: Begin indoors ten to twelve weeks early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp mix and keep it lit and moist during its slow start. This variety resists internal browning and holds a smooth, pale interior, rewarding steady moisture with round clean roots. Strip lower leaves late in the season and lift roots before the ground freezes.

planting.note: Start indoors six to eight weeks early and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors a half inch deep and move seedlings to deep, fertile ground with wide spacing. This Italian type is prized for tender, meaty stalks and is nearly spineless. Bundle and blanch the stalks for a few weeks before harvest, then cut and peel the ribs in cool autumn weather.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in early spring, or in fall in mild areas, spacing them widely.

directions: Set crowns with the buds an inch or two below the surface in deep, rich soil that drains well. It needs winter cold to thrive; resist harvesting the first year so the plant establishes, then in year two pull a portion of the stalks by twisting them free at the base. Remove flower stalks as they appear and never eat the poisonous leaves, only the red-green stems.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; this old variety can also be raised from seed.

directions: Plant crowns shallowly in fertile, well-drained soil, or start the seed indoors since this classic variety grows fairly true. Let plants build strength the first year, then harvest green-and-pink stalks in the second by twisting them off at the base. Cut off seed stalks promptly and discard the leaves, which are toxic.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in early spring; propagate only by division to keep the red color true.

directions: Set the crown buds an inch or two deep in rich, well-drained ground and water in well. This selection holds deep red color well into the stalk and stays sweet and tender. Skip harvest the first year, then pull the reddest stalks in year two and remove any flowering stems as they rise.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring and propagate by division to keep the strain uniform.

directions: Plant crowns shallowly in deep, fertile soil and give each a wide berth. This vigorous type produces thick, bright crimson stalks with good flavor. Let it establish the first season, then harvest by twisting stalks free in later years and always compost the leaves rather than eating them.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; this variety is one of the easiest to raise from seed.

directions: Start seed indoors early or plant crowns shallowly in rich soil, as this old variety comes reasonably true from seed. Its low-oxalic stalks allow a longer picking season than most, but still let plants settle the first year. Harvest stalks by twisting them off through spring and summer and remove flower stems as they form.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in early spring; divide established plants to propagate.

directions: Set crowns just below the surface in fertile, well-drained soil. This variety is famous for very early stalks and takes especially well to forcing under a cover for a tender, pale crop. Let it establish before harvesting hard, then pick stalks by twisting and discard the toxic leaves.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in a deep, rich trench.

directions: Set crowns in a trench with the roots fanned out and cover gradually as spears rise. This rust-resistant heirloom yields thick green spears and can also be grown from seed with patience. Wait two seasons before a full harvest, then cut spears for several weeks each spring and allow the summer ferns to recharge the bed.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; this all-male hybrid is best set from crowns.

directions: Plant crowns in a prepared trench, spreading the roots and backfilling as the spears grow up. This vigorous all-male hybrid puts energy into thick spears instead of seed and resists common rust and fungal diseases. Hold off harvesting the first two years, then cut spears each spring and let the ferns stand through summer.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in a deep trench in cold-winter regions.

directions: Set crowns over a low ridge in the bottom of a trench and fill in as growth extends upward. This early, all-male hybrid is cold-hardy and productive, favoring regions with real winters. Wait through two establishment years, then harvest spears in spring and leave the tall ferns to feed next year's crop.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in a rich, well-drained trench.

directions: Plant crowns in a trench with roots spread wide, covering them gradually as the spears emerge. This variety produces sweet, tender purple spears that are lower in fiber and good eaten raw, though the color turns green when cooked. Establish for two years before harvesting, then cut spears in spring and let ferns grow through summer.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; an exceptionally cold-hardy all-male hybrid.

directions: Set crowns in a trench, fanning out the roots and backfilling in stages as spears lengthen. This tough, high-yielding all-male hybrid tolerates heavy soils and severe winters better than most. Wait two years before full harvest, then cut spears each spring and allow the ferns to feed the long-lived bed.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; well suited to mild-winter and warmer regions.

directions: Plant crowns in a trench with roots spread over a mound and fill in as spears grow. This California-bred variety produces uniform, tight-tipped spears and performs well where winters are mild. Give it two years to settle, then harvest spears through spring and leave the ferns to stand until they yellow.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; a classic French heirloom often hilled for the rosy bases.

directions: Set crowns in a deep trench and cover gradually as the spears push up. This old French heirloom yields early, thick spears with rose-purple bases and can be raised from seed as well as crowns. Wait two seasons before harvesting fully, then cut spears in spring and let the summer ferns feed the roots.

planting.note: Sow once the soil is warm and settled a couple weeks after the last frost.

directions: Sow seeds about one inch deep in blocks of at least four short rows so the wind can pollinate the ears well. Keep the bed evenly moist and side-dress with nitrogen when the stalks are knee-high. Pick the classic yellow ears when the silks brown and the kernels spurt a milky juice when pressed.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost once soil holds above sixty-five degrees.

directions: Sow this sugary-enhanced bicolor an inch deep in a block, keeping it isolated from popcorn and field types to protect its sweetness. Feed lightly and keep moisture even during ear formation. The yellow-and-white ears are ready when the silks brown and tips fill out completely.

planting.note: Direct seed into warm ground after frost danger ends.

directions: Plant this sugary-enhanced yellow variety in short blocks for full pollination, sowing about an inch deep. Provide consistent water and a nitrogen boost as the stalks grow tall. Harvest the tender golden ears at the milk stage when silks have dried to brown.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in blocks and thin to a plant every foot to give the tall stalks room. This variety holds its sweetness well after picking, but still tastes best fresh. Gather the golden ears with reddish husk bases when the kernels are plump and the silks have browned.

planting.note: Sow when frost is past and soil is reliably warm.

directions: Plant this bicolor variety in short blocks rather than single long rows to ensure the ears fill out. Sow an inch deep, thin to a foot apart, and keep water steady through silking. Pick when the husks are dark green and the kernels release a milky juice when nicked.

planting.note: Direct seed after the last frost into warmed soil.

directions: Sow this large-eared sugary-enhanced yellow variety about an inch deep in a block. Give the vigorous stalks room and a mid-season nitrogen feeding. Harvest the big, full ears at the milk stage once silks have browned.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the danger of frost is gone.

directions: Plant this classic yellow variety an inch deep in blocks for good wind pollination. Keep the crop watered evenly and free of weeds while young. Pick the long, deep-kerneled ears when the silks dry and the kernels are plump.

planting.note: An early variety that can go in soon after the last frost.

directions: This quick, compact variety suits short seasons and small gardens; sow an inch deep in a tight block. Its shorter stalks still need even moisture and a foot of spacing. Harvest the early golden ears when silks brown, usually well ahead of most sweet corn.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Sow this tender sugary-enhanced yellow variety an inch deep in a block for reliable pollination. Keep moisture consistent and side-dress with nitrogen at knee height. Harvest the sweet, creamy ears at the milk stage when the silks have dried brown.

common_name: How Sweet It Is Sweet Corn

planting.note: A supersweet type that needs warm soil, so sow later than standard corn.

directions: This supersweet white variety germinates poorly in cool ground, so wait for soil above seventy degrees and sow an inch deep. Isolate it from other corn types to keep its extra-sweet kernels from turning starchy. Pick when silks brown and kernels are crisp and full.

planting.note: Supersweet corn that demands warm soil, so delay sowing until it heats up.

directions: Sow this yellow supersweet only once soil is thoroughly warm, placing seeds an inch deep in a block. Keep it isolated from standard and popcorn types to protect kernel quality. Harvest the crisp, very sweet ears when silks dry and kernels fill the tip.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warmed ground.

directions: A tall white heirloom known for holding at the eating stage a long time; sow seeds an inch deep in a block. Give the big stalks a foot of spacing and steady water through tasseling. Pick the long white ears at the milk stage when silks turn brown.

planting.note: Sow after frost and allow a long, warm season to fully mature the ears.

directions: Plant this compact popcorn an inch deep in a block, keeping it isolated from sweet corn so it pops true. Grow it to a full deep color, then let the small strawberry-shaped mahogany ears dry hard on the stalk. Cure the harvested ears indoors for several more weeks before shelling and popping.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil for a long popping-corn season.

directions: Also called White Rice, this hulless popcorn is sown an inch deep in an isolated block for pure popping quality. Let the ears mature and dry on the plant until the kernels are hard and glossy. Finish drying indoors, then shell for tender, nearly hull-free popcorn.

planting.note: Sow after frost; allow the full season for the dark ears to ripen.

directions: This short-season popcorn bears deep mahogany-black kernels; sow an inch deep in an isolated block. Let the ears dry fully on the plant, then cure them indoors. The dark kernels pop to a small tender white flake.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; its short stature suits small plots and short seasons.

directions: A very compact heirloom popcorn ideal for northern gardens; sow an inch deep in a small isolated block. The dwarf stalks each bear a couple of short ears that should dry on the plant. Cure indoors until kernels are hard, then shell and pop.

planting.note: Sow after frost and give it a long warm season to color up fully.

directions: This flint-type ornamental and popping corn produces jewel-like translucent kernels in many colors; sow an inch deep in a block. Grow it to full maturity and let the ears dry on tall stalks. Harvest for display when husks are papery, or shell the hard kernels to pop.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant flour corn that can go in soon after the last frost.

directions: Bred for short, cool seasons, this hardy multicolored flour-flint corn is sown an inch deep in a block. Its short, quick stalks tolerate rough conditions but still want steady moisture. Let the rainbow ears dry on the plant for grinding or ornament.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm soil for a full-season flint crop.

directions: This Italian heirloom flint bears deep red kernels prized for polenta; sow an inch deep in a block. Grow the tall stalks with even moisture and let ears dry fully on the plant. Shell the hard, glassy kernels once cured and grind for cornmeal.

planting.note: A northern flint that suits short seasons; sow soon after the last frost.

directions: This old Vermont flint corn shows red and yellow kernels and adapts to cool climates; sow an inch deep in a block. Give it even moisture and let the ears mature and dry on the stalks. Cure and shell the hard kernels for meal or seed.

planting.note: Sow after frost; allow a long season for the tall ears to fill.

directions: A New England yellow flint bearing long slender ears; sow an inch deep in a block for good pollination. Keep the tall stalks watered evenly through tasseling. Let the ears dry hard on the plant, then shell for cornmeal.

scientific_name: Zea mays var. indentata 'Reid's Yellow Dent'

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; this field corn needs a long warm season.

directions: A foundational American dent corn for meal and livestock; sow seeds an inch deep in a block. Grow the tall stalks with a nitrogen feeding and steady water through tasseling. Let the dented yellow kernels dry hard on the plant before shelling.

planting.note: Sow after frost; needs a long, warm growing season.

directions: This large-kerneled white dent is a Southern favorite for hominy and meal; sow an inch deep in a block. The tall stalks want rich soil, steady moisture, and room to spread. Harvest young for roasting ears, or let the big white kernels dry fully for grinding.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; this tall heirloom needs a full season.

directions: A striking heirloom dent with deep blood-red kernels; sow an inch deep in a block. The very tall stalks appreciate rich soil and consistent water through pollination. Pick young for roasting or let the red kernels dry hard for cornmeal.

scientific_name: Zea mays var. indentata 'Boone County White'

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm soil for a full-season field crop.

directions: An old white dent corn valued for meal and grits; sow seeds an inch deep in a block. Grow the tall stalks with even moisture and feed nitrogen mid-season. Let the white kernels dent and dry hard on the plant before shelling.

planting.note: Sow after frost; this drought-adapted flour corn takes a full season.

directions: This traditional Southwestern flour corn tolerates dry heat; sow an inch or two deep in a block. The soft-starch blue kernels grind easily once fully mature and dried on the stalk. Cure the ears indoors, then shell for a sweet blue meal.

planting.note: A northern flour corn suited to short seasons; sow soon after the last frost.

directions: This Northern Plains heirloom yields multicolored flour kernels on cool-adapted stalks; sow an inch deep in a block. Let the varied red, blue, and gold ears mature and dry on the plant. Shell the soft kernels after curing for meal or keep the ears for display.

planting.note: Sow after frost so the ears mature and dry by autumn for fall decoration.

directions: Grown for its multicolored flint kernels, this decorative corn is sown an inch deep in a block. Let the ears ripen fully and dry on the stalk until husks are papery. Harvest for autumn displays once the kernels are hard and richly colored.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: An old Southern roasting-ear corn with a mild starchy sweetness; sow an inch deep in a block. Grow the tall stalks with steady water and weed control while young. Pick the white ears young at the milk stage for the table, or let them dry for meal.

scientific_name: Zea mays var. everta 'Pennsylvania Dutch Butter'

planting.note: Sow after frost; allow the full season for the ears to dry down.

directions: A heirloom popcorn known for a rich, buttery flavor; sow an inch deep in an isolated block. Grow it to full maturity and let the yellow ears dry hard on the stalk. Cure indoors before shelling, then pop for a naturally buttery taste.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm soil for a full-season flint crop.

directions: An Italian heirloom flint traditionally ground for polenta; sow seeds an inch deep in a block. Keep the tall stalks watered evenly and let ears mature fully on the plant. Shell the hard, glassy kernels after curing and grind for a rich meal.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: This bicolor blends sugary-enhanced and supersweet genes for tender, sweet ears; sow an inch deep in a block. Keep moisture steady and give the plants a foot of spacing. Harvest the yellow-and-white ears at the milk stage when silks brown.

planting.note: Direct sow near last frost for its pink-to-cream mixed seed heads.

directions: Plant shallowly in cool, moist soil and thin seedlings to about a foot apart once they have several true leaves. This selection throws a rainbow of cream, rose, and cherry seed heads, so give it full sun to intensify the color. Cut heads when they crackle dry, thresh, and rinse the grain well to strip its natural coating.

planting.note: Sow in early spring for a vivid mix of colored seed heads by late summer.

directions: Scatter or drill seed a quarter inch deep after the ground warms and firm the soil for good contact. Expect a striking range of yellow, orange, pink, red, and purple panicles, which also make an ornamental cut display. Harvest grain once heads dry fully, then winnow and rinse to remove the coating before cooking.

planting.note: Wait for warm soil after frost danger, as seedlings are frost tender.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and barely cover it, keeping the top layer moist until seedlings appear. Amaranth thrives in summer heat and needs little water once its taproot is down, though thinning improves head size. Harvest when the plumes stop flowering and shed grain readily; cut heads, dry, then rub and winnow the seed clean.

planting.note: A short, uniform grain type; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow shallowly once soil is reliably warm and thin plants to reduce lodging in wind. This dependable grain selection stands more uniformly than many amaranths, making the pale seed easier to combine or hand-harvest. Cut the heads when dry and thresh over a tarp, then winnow off the chaff.

planting.note: Andean grain and ornamental; sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Press the small seed lightly into warm soil and keep the surface moist until sprouts show. Famous for its long, drooping crimson tassels, this species is grown both for grain and cut display, though pale-seeded strains eat best. Gather grain once the ropes of flowers dry and shed seed when handled.

planting.note: Large-seeded grain type; sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow the plump seed roughly an inch deep into a firm seedbed once frost has passed. This large-seeded selection is valued for grain yield and quick, uniform stands that shade out weeds. Harvest when the majority of seeds have browned and hardened, then cut, cure, and thresh before frost.

planting.note: Hardy dryland grain of the tropics; sow into warm soil.

directions: Plant seed shallowly into warm soil and keep it lightly moist until the grass takes hold. Kodo is prized for surviving poor, dry soils where other grains fail, producing hard seed that stores for years. Harvest when the heads dry fully, then thresh and hull the tough grain before cooking.

planting.note: Syrup-type cane sorghum; sow into warm soil for a long hot season.

directions: Plant seed about an inch deep in warm soil and space plants so the tall canes can stand without lodging. This sweet syrup type is grown chiefly for the sugary juice pressed from its stalks, though the seed heads still bear grain. Cut the canes when the seed reaches the hard-dough stage, strip the leaves, then press and cook the juice down into syrup.

planting.note: Long-brushed sorghum grown for craft fibers and seed; sow after frost.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in warm soil and give the tall stalks full sun and room to stand. Broomcorn is a sorghum selected for its long, stiff seed-head branches used to make brooms and crafts, while the grain feeds birds. Cut the panicles while the fibers are still green and pliable for crafts, or let them dry fully to save seed.

planting.note: High-altitude quinoa relative that prefers cool conditions; sow early.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly into cool, firm soil and thin lightly once seedlings establish. A hardy Andean relative of quinoa, kañiwa is shorter, more cold-tolerant, and its small seed needs no saponin rinsing. Harvest when the little seed heads dry, cutting whole plants before the crop shatters, then thresh and winnow.

planting.note: Aquatic annual; sow cold-stratified seed into shallow standing water in spring.

directions: Sow fresh seed that has been kept cold and wet directly into a foot or two of slow, clean standing water in spring. This aquatic grass roots in the mud and grows up through the water, so it needs a pond, ditch, or flooded bed rather than ordinary garden soil. Harvest in late summer by bending the ripe heads over a boat or bucket and knocking the dark grain loose, since it shatters as it ripens.

planting.note: Sow once the soil is thoroughly warm, a couple of weeks after the last frost.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow about one inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. Keep plants evenly watered through hot weather and pick often to keep them productive. Harvest the pods when they are two to four inches long and still tender, cutting every day or two.

planting.note: Plant in warm soil after frost danger has passed.

directions: Direct sow one inch deep in fertile, warm ground. This heat-loving variety tolerates dry spells well once established and bears reddish-orange pods. Harvest at three to five inches while the pods snap cleanly and remain tender.

planting.note: Set out transplants after all danger of frost, spacing them well for cross-pollination.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before setting out, then transplant deeply like a tomato. Plant at least two for good fruit set, since tomatillos need cross-pollination, and give them a cage or stake. Harvest when the papery green husks are filled out and begin to split.

planting.note: Set out after frost; this sprawling type appreciates room.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost into a spot with room to sprawl. This wild-type milpa strain bears small, tangy purple-tinged fruit and self-sows readily. Harvest as the husks fill and split, and expect volunteer seedlings the following year.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost date once soil warms.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep directly where plants are to grow, thinning to give each room. These towering single-stemmed plants often need staking in windy sites and produce large heads of striped, edible seeds. Harvest seed when the back of the head turns brown and the seeds are plump and loose.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost.

directions: Sow one inch deep once the soil is warm and settled. This heritage variety yields black-hulled seeds traditionally used as a dye, and the kernels are also edible. Harvest the heads when the backs brown, then dry them fully before threshing out the seed.

planting.note: Set out transplants near the last frost so young plants get a cool spell to trigger budding.

directions: Start seed indoors two to three months ahead and expose young plants to cool weather to encourage first-year buds. Grow in deep, rich soil with steady moisture, and in mild zones treat it as a returning perennial with winter mulch. Cut the flower buds while the scales are tight, before they begin to open.

planting.note: Transplant around last frost; this variety was bred to bud in its first year.

directions: Start indoors and grow on so plants receive a stretch of cool temperatures before setting out. Bred as an annual-cropping artichoke, it reliably produces buds the first season from an early start. Harvest the plump green heads while firm and closed, before the bracts loosen.

planting.note: Set out near the last frost date in deep, fertile soil.

directions: Start seed indoors well ahead and give the plants a cool period to prompt budding. This Italian type bears elongated purple-tinged buds and grows tall, so allow plenty of space. Cut the violet buds while the scales stay tight and glossy.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil after frost and give the vines a trellis.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then sow half an inch deep once the soil is warm. Provide a trellis for the twining red stems and keep the roots moist through heat. Pick the thick, glossy leaves and tender tips continually, which keeps the plant branching all summer.

planting.note: Sow the fine seed shallowly once soil warms after frost.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed and barely cover it, keeping the surface moist until sprouts appear. Harvest young leaves for salads or let plants grow tall for cooked greens and grain. Cut leaves as needed, or leave a few plants to form seed heads for edible grain in late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil a week or two after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep once the soil has warmed, since cold ground rots the beans. This early soybean sets its pods over a short window, so plan to harvest much of the crop at once. Pick when the pods are plump and bright green but before they yellow, then boil in the shell.

planting.note: Sow shelled seed into warm, loose soil after frost.

directions: Plant raw shelled kernels about one and a half inches deep in loose, sandy soil. After the yellow flowers fade, their stalks bend down and push pegs into the ground where the pods form. Harvest by lifting whole plants once the leaves yellow, then cure the pods in a warm, airy place.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep once the ground has warmed, avoiding cold wet soil. This larger-seeded soybean produces plump pods on sturdy bushy plants. Harvest when the pods are full and green, before any yellowing, and boil briefly in salted water in the shell.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow one inch deep in fully warm soil and full sun. This tall heirloom bears fat, deeply ribbed pods that show a star pattern when sliced across. Pick the pods young at three to four inches, since this variety toughens quickly if left on the plant.

planting.note: Inoculate pasteurized straw or hardwood sawdust indoors any time the room stays cool and humid.

directions: Mix grain spawn evenly through pasteurized straw or supplemented sawdust and pack it into a breathable bag or column. Hold the colonizing block near 65 to 75 degrees in the dark for two to three weeks, then cut fruiting slits and raise humidity with frequent misting and fresh air. Harvest whole clusters when the caps flatten out but before the edges begin to curl upward and drop spores.

planting.note: A cold-loving oyster strain best inoculated when indoor temperatures sit in the low sixties.

directions: Inoculate pasteurized straw or hardwood sawdust and colonize in the dark around 60 to 68 degrees. Introduce cool fresh air and steady humidity to trigger the deep blue-grey pins, which fade to grey as they expand. Cut the clusters at the base while the caps are still slightly convex for the best texture.

planting.note: A tropical, fast oyster that wants warm rooms in the seventies and eighties.

directions: Colonize supplemented straw or sawdust at warm room temperature, then open the block to bright indirect light and high humidity. Bright pink clusters form quickly and mature within a few days, so watch them closely. Harvest young while the color is vivid, because older flushes toughen and fade rapidly.

planting.note: A warm-climate yellow oyster that fruits in loose bouquet clusters.

directions: Run grain spawn into pasteurized straw or hardwood sawdust and colonize around 70 to 80 degrees. Expose the block to indirect light and humid fresh air to draw out the bright yellow bouquets. Pick the whole cluster when caps just flatten, since this species is delicate and short-lived once mature.

planting.note: A thick-stemmed oyster best fruited from supplemented sawdust blocks.

directions: Inoculate supplemented hardwood sawdust and fully colonize the block over three to four weeks near 65 to 70 degrees. Fruit through a small opening with high humidity and moderate fresh air so a few large mushrooms form rather than many small ones. Harvest when the caps flatten but before the gills fully open, keeping the meaty stem intact.

planting.note: A summer oyster tolerant of warmer rooms than the pearl strain.

directions: Mix spawn into pasteurized straw or sawdust and colonize at warm room temperatures. Cut fruiting holes and maintain high humidity with regular airflow to raise pale tan clusters. Harvest the whole shelf when the caps broaden but the margins are still slightly incurved.

planting.note: Inoculate freshly cut hardwood logs in spring while sap is running.

directions: Drill rows of holes in fresh oak or other dense hardwood logs, tap in spawn plugs, and seal each hole with cheese wax. Stack the logs in deep shade and keep them from drying out while the mycelium colonizes over six to eighteen months. Force fruiting by soaking a colonized log in cold water for a day, then harvest the domed caps once the veils break and the edges curl under.

planting.note: A toothed fungus fruited from supplemented hardwood sawdust blocks or logs.

directions: Inoculate supplemented hardwood sawdust and colonize in the dark around 65 to 70 degrees for two to four weeks. Cut a single fruiting slit and keep humidity very high with gentle airflow so one rounded, icicle-toothed cluster forms. Harvest while the spines are short and white, before they lengthen and turn pinkish or yellow with age.

planting.note: Layer spawn into an outdoor bed of fresh hardwood chips in spring or fall.

directions: Build a shaded bed of fresh hardwood chips or straw a few inches deep and layer grain or sawdust spawn between the material. Keep the bed damp through the season while the white mycelium spreads, topping up chips each year. Large burgundy-capped mushrooms push up in warm, wet weather; harvest them young before the caps flatten and the gills darken.

planting.note: Grown indoors on pasteurized composted manure and straw with a casing layer.

directions: Mix spawn through cooled, pasteurized composted straw and manure and let it colonize warm and dark for about two weeks. Add a moist casing layer of peat and lime, then drop the temperature and raise humidity to trigger pinning. Harvest white buttons while closed for classic mushrooms, or leave them to open and brown into cremini and portobello before the gills fully expose.

planting.note: A cold-fruiting mushroom grown in bottles or jars for long pale stems.

directions: Colonize supplemented sawdust in tall containers, then move them to a cold, dark, humid space near 45 degrees with restricted airflow. The low temperature and high carbon dioxide draw the stems up long and thin with small white caps. Harvest the dense bundle when the stems reach the desired length but before the caps broaden.

planting.note: Fruited from buried supplemented sawdust blocks or inoculated oak logs.

directions: Inoculate supplemented hardwood sawdust and let it fully colonize and firm up over several weeks in the dark. Bury the block partway in shaded soil or sand and keep it consistently moist as cool autumn weather approaches. A large rosette of overlapping grey fronds emerges at the surface; harvest the whole cluster while the frond tips are still rounded and firm.

planting.note: A warm-loving shelf fungus grown on supplemented hardwood sawdust or logs.

directions: Colonize supplemented hardwood sawdust and fruit it warm, around 75 to 85 degrees, with high humidity. Low fresh air produces antler-like growths, while ample airflow flattens the growth into the classic lacquered red conk. Harvest the tough conk once its white growing margin fully colors in, then dry it for use.

planting.note: A cool-season log or sawdust mushroom prized for its glossy gelatinous caps.

directions: Inoculate hardwood logs or supplemented sawdust and colonize in shade over several months. Trigger fruiting with the cool, damp weather of autumn, keeping humidity high. Harvest the small amber caps while they still carry their signature slippery gloss, before the veils fully tear.

planting.note: A crunchy-stemmed cluster mushroom grown on supplemented hardwood sawdust.

directions: Run spawn into supplemented hardwood sawdust and colonize in the dark for three to four weeks. Open the block and fruit it cool and humid with steady fresh air to bring on tight clusters of shiny brown caps. Harvest the whole bunch when the caps are still rounded, keeping the crisp stems attached.

planting.note: A slow, dense-clustering mushroom grown in bottles of supplemented sawdust.

directions: Colonize supplemented hardwood sawdust in containers and allow a long maturation period before fruiting. Move to a cold, humid environment to initiate the tightly packed clusters with their marbled caps. Harvest the entire clump when the caps are firm and just beginning to separate.

planting.note: An orange club fungus grown in jars on a cooked grain and nutrient broth.

directions: Inoculate a sterilized grain-and-broth substrate in jars and colonize in the dark at cool room temperature. Move the jars into indirect light to trigger the bright orange fingerlike stromata, keeping humidity high. Harvest the clubs once they reach full length and deep color, before they begin to release spores.

planting.note: A clustering brown-capped mushroom fruited on supplemented hardwood sawdust.

directions: Colonize supplemented hardwood sawdust in the dark for three to four weeks at warm room temperature. Open the block and fruit with high humidity and good fresh air to draw up slender clusters with domed brown caps and pale stems. Harvest the whole bunch while the caps are still convex and the ring is intact.

planting.note: A fast tropical mushroom grown on beds of composted rice straw in heat.

directions: Build beds of wet, pasteurized rice straw and layer in spawn while the material is still warm. Keep the bed hot and humid, near 85 to 95 degrees, and the egg-shaped pins appear within a week. Harvest at the button or egg stage while the volva still encloses the young mushroom, before the cap breaks free and opens.

planting.note: A warm-season relative of the button mushroom with a sweet almond aroma.

directions: Spawn a pasteurized compost of straw and manure and colonize it warm and dark for a couple of weeks. Add a moist casing layer and hold warm temperatures with high humidity to bring on the tan, scaly caps. Harvest while the caps are still closed or just opening, when the almond scent is strongest.

planting.note: A hardy bracket fungus grown on inoculated hardwood logs or sawdust blocks.

directions: Inoculate hardwood logs with plug spawn or fruit supplemented sawdust blocks, keeping the substrate shaded and damp. Thin, banded, fan-shaped brackets form along the wood in cool, humid weather. Harvest the leathery fans while their outer growing edge is still pale and firm, then dry them for use.

planting.note: Transplant after the last spring frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Sow indoors and grow on strong transplants before setting them deep after frost. This tall potato-leaf indeterminate resists cracking and rewards steady watering and a firm trellis. Gather the large pink fruit when it ripens to a rich rose and the shoulders soften.

planting.note: Move outdoors after the last frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow in warm flats and transplant vigorous seedlings deeply after frost. This tall indeterminate carries heavy orange beefsteaks, so cage it well and water evenly to prevent splitting. Harvest when the fruit glows deep orange throughout and the base gives slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Start seed shallowly indoors and set the potato-leaf transplant out deeply after frost. This indeterminate produces large pink beefsteaks best supported on a tall stake and watered evenly. Gather the fruit when it ripens to a deep rose and gives to light pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Grow transplants in warm flats and plant them deeply after the last frost. This dependable indeterminate sets large smooth red beefsteaks and appreciates consistent watering. Pick the fruit when it colors a deep even red and softens slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; begin seed indoors about six weeks ahead.

directions: Sow indoors and set the potato-leaf transplant out deeply once frost has passed. This earlier Brandywine-type indeterminate bears flattened pink beefsteaks and needs firm staking. Harvest when the fruit deepens to rosy pink and yields to a gentle press.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow shallowly indoors and plant the transplant deep after frost. A green-fleshed selection from the Cherokee family, this indeterminate ripens to amber-green rather than red, so judge it by softness. Harvest when the skin turns yellow-green and the fruit gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant vigorous plants deeply after frost. This tall indeterminate carries large golden-yellow beefsteaks and benefits from strong caging and rich soil. Pick when the fruit ripens to a deep glowing yellow and softens.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; begin indoors about six weeks ahead.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the transplant deep after the last frost. This old prize-winning indeterminate bears deep orange-gold slicers on a tall vine that needs support. Harvest when the fruit colors a rich orange and gives slightly at the base.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply once frost has passed. This Russian indeterminate makes large low-acid orange beefsteaks that ripen to a persimmon hue. Harvest when the fruit turns deep golden-orange and softens at the blossom end.

planting.note: Set out after frost; begin indoors roughly six weeks ahead.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant the transplant deep after frost. This Swiss indeterminate bears smooth medium pink slicers that resist cracking with even watering. Pick when the fruit ripens to a clear rose-pink and gives slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Sow indoors and set out sturdy transplants after frost. This French semi-determinate produces ribbed red slicing fruit on a compact bush that needs only light staking. Harvest the somewhat flattened, lobed fruit when it colors a full red and softens.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Grow transplants indoors and plant them deep after frost. This classic 1890s indeterminate bears large meaty pink beefsteaks with few seeds and needs strong caging. Pick when the fruit ripens to deep pink and yields at the base.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; begin indoors about six weeks ahead.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply after the last frost. Known for record-setting fruit, this indeterminate needs generous spacing, rich soil, and heavy support. Thin to a few fruit per cluster and harvest each giant red beefsteak when it colors fully and softens.

planting.note: Set out after frost; sow indoors roughly six weeks before.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant sturdy transplants deep after frost. This 1920s Illinois indeterminate bears clusters of large deep-red slicing fruit and wants firm support. Harvest when the fruit reaches a rich even red and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and set the potato-leaf transplant deep after frost. This heart-shaped pink indeterminate has open foliage, so watch the heavy fruit and support the vine well. Pick the large oxheart fruit when it deepens to rosy pink and softens.

planting.note: Set out after frost; begin indoors roughly six weeks ahead.

directions: Grow transplants indoors and plant deep after frost. This oxheart-type indeterminate bears large strawberry-shaped red fruit with meaty, low-seed flesh. Harvest when the fruit colors a full red and yields slightly at the point.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the transplant out deeply after frost. This indeterminate from Lebanon produces very large sweet pink beefsteaks that need strong caging and steady water. Cut the fruit when it ripens to a deep pink and softens.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost, spacing plants widely. This indeterminate produces very heavy dark-pink beefsteaks, so thin the clusters and provide the sturdiest support you can. Harvest each giant fruit when it colors fully and gives to firm pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; sow indoors roughly six weeks before.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant sturdy plants deep after frost. This early 1900s indeterminate bears uniform medium red slicing fruit in reliable clusters and wants steady support. Harvest when the fruit reaches a full even red and softens slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out transplants after frost. This dependable determinate forms a compact bush of smooth red globe fruit and needs only light staking. Pick the fruit when it colors a full red and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; begin indoors roughly six weeks ahead.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply after the last frost. This old canning-and-slicing indeterminate bears firm smooth red fruit that stores and ships well; support it with a cage. Harvest when the fruit colors a solid red and yields slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant the potato-leaf transplant deep after frost. This Balkan indeterminate bears large pink beefsteaks and needs firm caging and even moisture. Harvest when the fruit deepens to rosy pink and softens at the base.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost. This German-heritage indeterminate bears smooth round pink-purple fruit that resists disease and cracking. Harvest the medium slicers when they color a clean rosy pink and give slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; begin indoors about six weeks ahead.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant the potato-leaf transplant deep after frost. This Virginia indeterminate produces large golden beefsteaks and needs strong caging and steady feeding. Harvest when the fruit turns a rich gold and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; sow indoors roughly six weeks before.

directions: Sow indoors early and transplant the potato-leaf seedling deep after frost. This late-maturing Tennessee indeterminate bears large smooth pale-yellow beefsteaks, so give it a long warm season and firm support. Harvest when the fruit ripens to a soft yellow and yields at the base.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant deep after frost. This 1880s indeterminate bears smooth golden-yellow slicing fruit sometimes blushed pink at the blossom end. Harvest when the fruit colors a full gold and gives slightly.

planting.note: Set out after frost; begin indoors roughly six weeks ahead.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after the last frost. This indeterminate performs well in cooler summers and bears smooth deep-red slicing fruit. Harvest when the fruit colors fully and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant the potato-leaf transplant deep after frost. This Polish-heritage indeterminate bears rich-flavored pink beefsteaks and wants firm support. Harvest when the fruit deepens to rose-pink and gives slightly at the base.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost. This West Virginia indeterminate bears large slightly ribbed red beefsteaks and needs a tall cage and even water. Harvest when the fruit colors a full red and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; sow indoors roughly six weeks before.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after the last frost. This indeterminate from the Caspian region bears large sweet pink beefsteaks and needs strong caging and steady water. Harvest when the fruit ripens to a deep pink and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant the potato-leaf transplant deep after frost. This yellow-fruited Brandywine indeterminate bears large golden-orange beefsteaks and wants strong support and even moisture. Harvest when the fruit turns deep gold and softens at the base.

planting.note: Set out after frost; begin indoors roughly six weeks ahead.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost. This reddish-orange Brandywine selection is a productive indeterminate that holds up better in heat than the pink type. Harvest when the fruit colors a deep orange-red and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant deep after frost. This indeterminate bears large pink-red beefsteaks on a tall vine that needs sturdy support and even water. Harvest when the fruit deepens to a dusky pink-red and softens at the base.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost. This reliable indeterminate bears heavy crops of medium-large red slicing fruit even in heat, so keep it well supported. Harvest when the fruit colors a full even red and gives slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; begin indoors about six weeks ahead.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out transplants after frost. This 1930s early indeterminate produces smooth medium red globes and gives a dependable earlier harvest. Pick the fruit when it colors a full red and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; start indoors about six weeks earlier.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant deep after frost. This indeterminate bears large meaty red beefsteaks and rewards a tall cage and even water. Harvest when the fruit colors a full deep red and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; begin indoors roughly six weeks ahead.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost, spacing plants generously. This indeterminate bears huge heart-shaped pink-red slicing fruit that needs the sturdiest support you can give. Harvest when the fruit colors fully and softens at the point.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; sow indoors about six weeks before.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant deep after frost. This Azorean indeterminate bears large slightly ribbed red beefsteaks on a vigorous vine that needs strong caging. Harvest when the fruit colors a full red and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Set out after frost; start indoors roughly six weeks earlier.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deep after frost. This French indeterminate bears large ribbed red beefsteaks and sets well even in cooler conditions, so support it firmly. Harvest when the fruit colors a full red and softens at the base.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; begin indoors about six weeks ahead.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant the potato-leaf transplant deep after frost. This Brandywine-related indeterminate bears large pink beefsteaks and wants a tall cage and even moisture. Harvest when the fruit deepens to rosy pink and gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Start indoors and set hardened seedlings out once night temperatures stay above 50F.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm flats and pot up before transplanting to a staked or caged position. Feed steadily and keep moisture even to prevent the large heart-shaped fruit from cracking or splitting at the shoulders. Pick when the meaty, nearly seedless fruit colors fully red and yields slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Begin seed under lights and move plants outdoors after frost danger passes.

directions: Transplant deeply, burying the lower stem to encourage extra roots on the tall indeterminate vines. Support the heavy pink-red hearts with strong stakes and prune to a couple of leaders for larger fruit. Harvest when the pointed fruit ripens to a deep rosy red and the flesh feels dense.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Set young plants deep and cage or trellis them, since the fruit can weigh well over a pound. Keep watering consistent and mulch to steady soil moisture, which reduces blossom-end rot on the big hearts. Gather the meaty, low-seed fruit as it turns fully red and softens slightly.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant after nights warm and frost has passed.

directions: Grow this tall indeterminate on a sturdy stake and prune suckers for well-sized hearts. The heart-shaped fruit ripens orange with pink-red streaking through the sweet, fruity flesh. Pick when the shoulders take on full color and the fruit gives gently under light pressure.

planting.note: Sow under cover early and set out well-hardened plants after frost.

directions: Transplant deeply and provide strong support for the large, dense oxheart fruit. Water evenly and feed through the season to size up the meaty, crack-resistant hearts. Harvest when the deep red fruit fills out and softens slightly at the tip.
